locked_facts: Faraday's law: ε = -NdΦ/dt (induced EMF = negative rate of change of flux)
Magnetic flux: Φ = BAcosθ (B = field, A = area, θ = angle between B and area normal)
EMF can be induced by changing B, A, or θ
The negative sign represents Lenz's law — induced EMF opposes the change
No change in flux = no induced EMF (static field through stationary coil gives zero EMF)
Faster change in flux = larger induced EMF
Motional EMF: ε = BLv for a conductor of length L moving at velocity v perpendicular to B
truth_statements: An EMF is induced whenever the magnetic flux through a circuit changes with time
The magnitude of induced EMF equals the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage
The induced EMF can be increased by increasing the rate of change of flux — faster motion or faster field change
A static magnetic field through a stationary coil produces zero EMF — flux must CHANGE
Faraday's law is the fundamental principle behind generators, transformers, and inductors

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three ways to change flux shown simultaneously: (1) move magnet toward coil (changing B at coil), (2) expand coil area (changing A), (3) rotate coil in field (changing θ). All produce EMF. The common thread: dΦ/dt ≠ 0.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three animations with flux vs time graphs — different methods but same principle: changing flux = EMF
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think only moving a magnet induces EMF — seeing three distinct mechanisms unified by dΦ/dt deepens understanding

simulation_states.label: Flux defined
simulation_states.description: A coil in a magnetic field. Flux Φ = BAcosθ shown. Rotating the coil changes θ, changing flux. When Φ is constant, no EMF.
simulation_states.emphasis: Flux is B × A × cosθ — it depends on field, area, AND orientation

simulation_states.label: Magnet moving into coil
simulation_states.description: A bar magnet moves toward a coil. Flux increases. Galvanometer deflects. Moving magnet away: flux decreases, deflection reverses. Stationary magnet: zero EMF.
simulation_states.emphasis: Changing flux induces EMF. No change = no EMF.

simulation_states.label: Three ways to change flux
simulation_states.description: Side by side: (1) change B (magnet motion), (2) change A (coil expanding), (3) change θ (coil rotating). All produce EMF. Flux-time graphs shown for each.
simulation_states.emphasis: It's always dΦ/dt — doesn't matter HOW the flux changes

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student controls magnet speed, coil turns, and coil area. EMF and flux graphs update in real time. Faster magnet = larger EMF. More turns = larger EMF.
simulation_states.emphasis: Speed up the magnet — EMF increases. Add more turns — EMF increases.

common_misconceptions.belief: A magnetic field through a coil always produces EMF
common_misconceptions.correction: A static (unchanging) magnetic flux produces ZERO EMF. The flux must be CHANGING (dΦ/dt ≠ 0) for EMF to be induced. A coil sitting in a constant field has zero EMF.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show coil in constant B field with galvanometer reading zero — then move the magnet to show EMF appears

common_misconceptions.belief: Induced EMF depends on the total flux, not its rate of change
common_misconceptions.correction: EMF depends on how FAST the flux changes (dΦ/dt), not on the total flux value. A large constant flux gives zero EMF. A small but rapidly changing flux gives large EMF.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show large constant flux (zero EMF) vs small rapidly changing flux (large EMF)
